variation.emphasis: Lead with the strongest legal-sufficiency point and keep it tight.

advocate_args: no facts on conditions precedent or damages
group pleading and no scienter basis

transcript.advocate_opening:
Your Honor, this Complaint fails at the threshold because it never pleads the essential facts that would make either claim plausible.

Start with the contract claim. In this Circuit, a plaintiff must do more than say there was a contract, it was breached, and harm followed. The Complaint never identifies the contractual provision Defendants supposedly violated. That omission is fatal. Without a pleaded term, the Court cannot assess breach; it is left with a legal conclusion dressed up as an allegation. The pleading defect is compounded by Plaintiff’s failure to allege facts showing satisfaction of any condition precedent and by its wholly abstract damages allegations. Under Twombly and Iqbal, that is not a plausible contract claim. It is a formulaic recital of elements.

The fraud claim is even more clearly deficient. Rule 9(b) demands the who, what, when, where, and how of the alleged fraud. The Complaint pleads none of that. It does not identify who made the statements, when or where they were made, how they were conveyed, or what makes them false in a particularized way. Scienter is pleaded only by attaching the labels “knowing” and “intentional,” which is precisely what Rule 9(b) does not permit. And the problem is exacerbated by impermissible group pleading against all Defendants collectively, which cannot substitute for particularized allegations as to each speaker and each misrepresentation.

So even taking the allegations as true, the Complaint does not cross the line from conceivable to plausible on contract, and it falls well short of Rule 9(b) on fraud. Given that Plaintiff has already amended once and still has not cured these substantive defects, dismissal of the fraud claim with prejudice is warranted, and the contract claim should be dismissed as well.

ARGUMENTS: No pleaded contractual breach term; No facts on conditions precedent or damages; Rule 9(b) particularity failure; Group pleading and no scienter basis
transcript.opponent_attack:
The motion fails at the threshold because it never ties its Rule 12(b)(6) conclusions to the actual allegations of this Complaint. A dismissal motion must show that, even taking the pleaded facts as true, one or more required elements are absent. Defendants do not do that. On the contract claim, they say Plaintiff did not identify the “specific contractual provision,” but Rule 8 does not require a complaint to quote chapter and verse from the contract so long as it plausibly alleges the agreement, the parties’ obligations, Plaintiff’s performance or excuse, Defendants’ nonperformance, and resulting damages. That is exactly what the Complaint does. Just as important, their “conditions precedent” argument ignores Rule 9(c): conditions precedent may be alleged generally. Defendants identify no allegation on the face of the Complaint that defeats satisfaction or excuse of any condition precedent, so that point is not a basis for dismissal.

Their fraud argument has the same defect. They recite Rule 9(b) at a high level, but they do not actually engage the pleaded allegations defendant by defendant and statement by statement. They do not show that the Complaint lacks the basic who, what, when, where, and how; they only insist Plaintiff should have pleaded more detail. That is not a merits defect. And even if the Court wanted greater precision as to attribution, timing, or wording, that is the textbook definition of a curable pleading issue—not a reason to dismiss with prejudice, especially where Defendants offer no complaint-specific analysis showing futility.

In short, this motion is long on slogans and short on pleading analysis. It does not identify a missing element, does not overcome Rule 9(c), and does not justify the extraordinary request for prejudice.

ATTACK: no complaint-specific deficiency shown
transcript.bench_pressure_points: Counsel, your argument is pitched at a high level of generality. On the contract claim, I need you to point me to the actual allegations in the Complaint and explain element by element what is missing—particularly why the failure to quote a “specific provision” is fatal here, given Rule 8 and Rule 9(c)’s allowance that conditions precedent may be alleged generally. On the fraud claim, the same problem appears: you recite Rule 9(b), but unless you engage the pleaded statements, speakers, timing, and attribution as actually alleged, you are asking me to dismiss based on an abstraction rather than a demonstrated pleading defect.
transcript.advocate_rebuttal:
Respectfully, this is not a demand for “chapter and verse”; it is a demand that the Complaint identify the contractual obligation allegedly breached, and here it does not—it alleges breach in the abstract, without tying Defendants’ supposed nonperformance to any pleaded term, without alleging any facts showing Plaintiff performed or was excused from performing the prerequisites to enforceability, and without pleading concrete damages beyond conclusory harm. Rule 9(c) permits general averment of conditions precedent, but it does not excuse their complete omission as a factual matter where, as here, the pleading never states that Plaintiff satisfied or was excused from satisfying the preconditions to Defendants’ duty.

The fraud count fails for the same reason: the Complaint does not plead the who, what, when, where, or how of any misrepresentation, instead attributing unspecified “false statements” to “Defendants” collectively and pleading scienter only through labels like “knowing” and “intentional.” That is not a request for more detail; it is the absence of the particularized allegations Rule 9(b) requires, and after a prior amendment, dismissal with prejudice is warranted.
transcript.bench_final:
The motion is granted in substantial part: the fraud claim is dismissed, with prejudice, because the pleading described in the papers does not come close to Rule 9(b)’s requirement of particularized allegations as to the speaker, content, timing, and basis for scienter; and the contract claim is also dismissed because Plaintiff never identifies the contractual obligation allegedly breached or pleads performance/excuse except at the level of conclusion. If Plaintiff has not already had a fair opportunity to cure the contract pleading, that dismissal is without prejudice; otherwise it is with prejudice as well.

DECISIVE: the Complaint, as characterized by both sides, pleads breach and fraud at the level of labels and conclusions rather than tying either claim to the required element-by-element facts.
